did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically an EfficientNetV2 neuronal network, to classify and localize anatomical tissue structures and neoplasms of the skin (including basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma, and naevi) on scanned histopathological tissue sections.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents original experimental work involving the manual annotation of 386 cases, extraction of 129,364 image tiles, training of an EfficientNetV2 model, and evaluation on internal and external test sets to achieve high diagnostic accuracy.

title: Deep learning for the detection of anatomical tissue structures and neoplasms of the skin on scanned histopathological tissue sections
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning model to classify and localize anatomical tissue structures and common skin neoplasms, including basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma, and naevi, on scanned histopathological slides. The researchers manually annotated 16 classes across 386 cases, extracting 129,364 image tiles to train an EfficientNetV2 neuronal network. Model performance was evaluated using cross-entropy loss, balanced accuracy, and Matthews correlation coefficient on internal validation and test sets, achieving high diagnostic accuracies of 98% and 97%, respectively. Additionally, the model was applied to an external dataset of melanoma whole slides to assess its localization capabilities under varying staining and scanning conditions. The results demonstrate that automated differentiation of skin tumors and non-tumor structures is feasible with high accuracy, supporting its potential to improve routine dermatopathology diagnostics.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 16 manually annotated classes; n=93 cases in training dataset; n=71 training, n=12 validation, n=10 test patients.
One of the 16 manually annotated classes; n=100 cases in training dataset; n=61 training, n=10 validation, n=29 test patients.
One of the 16 manually annotated classes; n=87 cases in training dataset; n=59 training, n=9 validation, n=19 test patients. Also evaluated on an external test set of 41 cases.
One of the 16 manually annotated classes; n=98 cases in training dataset; n=72 training, n=8 validation, n=18 test patients.

all_task_types.target: 16 classes of anatomical tissue structures and neoplasms (including BCC, SqCC, melanoma, naevi, and non-tumor structures like dermis, epidermis, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The model classifies image tiles into discrete diagnostic categories. The study reports balanced accuracy (98% validation, 97% test) and Matthews correlation coefficient for class prediction. It also performs localization of melanoma and non-tumor tissue on whole slides without prior annotation, but the core AI task is multi-class classification of tissue types.

datasets_used.name: Internal Dataset (Heidelberg/Trier/Hannover)
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of scanned histopathological tissue sections derived from the archives of the Institute of Pathology, Heidelberg University; the MVZ for Histology, Cytology and Molecular Diagnostics Trier; and the Institute for Dermatopathology Hannover. Slides were scanned at 400x magnification. The dataset includes 16 manually annotated classes covering major non-tumor anatomical tissue structures and major skin tumor categories. Data is publicly available via HeiDATA.

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNetV2 S model with random augmentation.
use.validation: Used for model optimization, hyperparameter tuning, and selecting the best performing model based on MCC.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model's diagnostic accuracy and confusion matrices.
use.other: Publicly released to enable other researchers to train, validate, and improve deep learning models.

datasets_used.name: External Test Set (CPTAC-CM)
datasets_used.description: An independent external dataset of melanoma whole slides downloaded from the Cancer Imaging Archive (CPTAC-CM). The slides were scanned at 200x magnification (different from the internal 400x) and exhibited varying staining properties and artifacts. 62 cases passed initial quality control, but only 41 cases (32 melanoma, 9 tumor-free skin) were used for final analysis after manual review.

use.testing: Used to assess the model's localization capabilities and performance under suboptimal input data conditions (varying staining, scanning conditions, and artifacts).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess concordance between actual and predicted classes on the validation and test sets, and to evaluate misclassification rates between specific classes (e.g., naevus vs. melanoma, SqCC vs. BCC).
To evaluate and compare model performance during training and optimization, specifically to select the best performing model.
To evaluate model performance, optimize hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size), and select the final model configuration based on validation data.
To evaluate model performance during training and optimization.
To perform dimension reduction and visualize the proximity/overlap of image classes in the validation and test data.
To render a final diagnosis on a whole slide level by aggregating image patch-based results.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as balanced accuracy, Matthews correlation coefficient, cross-entropy loss, and confusion matrices.

Internal Validation Set.notes: The abstract states a high diagnostic accuracy of 98% in the validation set. Table 2 shows the best model based on MCC achieved a validation BAC of 0.93590 and validation MCC of 0.93590. The text in section 3.2 notes high concordance but highlights specific misclassifications: naevus misclassified as melanoma (2.75%) and vice versa (4.18%); SqCC misclassified as BCC (8.81%) and vice versa (1.51%); elastosis (33.33%), vessels (9.56%), and nerves (5.94%) misclassified as dermis; epidermis misclassified as SqCC (5.43%) and vice versa (3.70%).

Internal Test Set.notes: The abstract states a high diagnostic accuracy of 97% in the test set. Section 3.3 states that on the case level, 80 out of 81 tumor patients had the correct diagnosis voted by image tiles, resulting in a final diagnostic accuracy of 98.7%. Two patients with squamous cell carcinomas were misclassified. Non-tumor skin cases had generally a proportion of tiles predicted as tumor <5%.

External Test Set (CPTAC-CM).notes: Applied to 41 cases (32 melanoma, 9 tumor-free skin). Of the 41 melanoma cases, 32 were predicted as melanoma (78%). The text notes a decreased performance of 78% for melanoma and 84% in all whole slides on the external test set considering a majority vote. The external slides were scanned at 200x magnification with varying staining and artifacts.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet reference dataset (implied by citation 17 and context of EfficientNetV2 capabilities).
architecture_used.input_size: Variable due to progressive training: 128 x 128 px (first 15 epochs), 300 x 300 px (last 15 epochs for S model); 128 x 128 px (first 15 epochs), 380 x 380 px (last 15 epochs for M model).

architecture_details.training_strategy: Progressive learning (increasing input size and regularization over epochs)
architecture_details.augmentation: Random augmentation (RandAugment) with n=2, M=5 (first 15 epochs), M=15 (last 15 epochs for S); M=20 (last 15 epochs for M)

training_details.optimizer: AMSGrad (variant of Adam with b1=0.9, b2=0.999, epsilon=1.0*10^-7)

learning_rate.schedule: Linear warm-up for first 5 epochs, then exponential decay with rate 0.97

training_details.hardware: Amazon Web Services p3.2xlarge instance with single V100 GPU
training_details.software: TensorFlow Docker container, Singularity container, Python umap-learn

preprocessing_techniques: Image patch extraction: 100 x 100 µm (~395 x 395 px) tiles extracted from scanned histopathological slides using QuPath.
Quality control/Filtering: Blurry images were deleted during patch review.

Quality control/Filtering: External test set slides underwent manual review and quality control to filter out cases with high burdens of artifacts (blurry areas, tissue tears, dust particles, high necrosis, low staining quality).
Random augmentation: Applied during training using RandAugment (n=2) with varying M values (5, 15, or 20) to reduce overfitting.

Progressive training: Input image size increased during training (e.g., from 128x128 px to 300x300 px for EfficientNetV2 S, or 128x128 px to 380x380 px for EfficientNetV2 M) to improve training speed and performance.
Class balancing: Data sampling was adjusted during training to ensure no class imbalance.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to reduce overfitting with n=2. The parameter M varied by training stage and model size: M=5 for the first 15 epochs (all models), M=15 for the last 15 epochs for EfficientNetV2 S, and M=20 for the last 15 epochs for EfficientNetV2 M.
Data was sampled during training to ensure no class imbalance.
Input image size was increased during training to improve speed and performance: from 128x128 px to 300x300 px for EfficientNetV2 S, and from 128x128 px to 380x380 px for EfficientNetV2 M.

conclusions: Automated differentiation of basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SqCC), melanoma, naevi, and non-tumor tissue structures is feasible using deep learning techniques.
The EfficientNetV2 model achieved high diagnostic accuracy, with balanced accuracy rates of 98% on the internal validation set and 97% on the internal test set.
On the case level, the model achieved a final diagnostic accuracy of 98.7% (80 out of 81 tumor patients) on the internal test set.
The model demonstrated the ability to localize melanoma and non-tumor tissue on whole slides without prior annotation.

Performance decreased when applied to an external test set with varying staining, scanning conditions, and artifacts, achieving a melanoma detection rate of 78% and 84% accuracy across all whole slides.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the number of patients and entities included, noting that the full morphological spectrum of the tumors could not be fully represented.
The application of this deep learning model is recommended only as a diagnostic supplement and should always be conducted under the supervision of an expert pathologist.
The curated dataset and model are provided publicly to enable other researchers to train, validate, and improve deep learning models for dermatopathology.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the number of patients and entities included, noting that the full morphological spectrum of the tumors could not be fully represented.

Performance decreased significantly on the external test set (CPTAC-CM) due to varying staining, scanning conditions, and artifacts, with melanoma detection dropping to 78% compared to 97% on the internal test set.
The external test set slides were scanned at 200x magnification, differing from the internal dataset's 400x magnification, which contributed to performance degradation.

The model exhibited specific misclassifications: BCC and SqCC were confused due to overlapping morphological features (squamous differentiation in BCC, basaloid appearance in SqCC); naevi and melanoma were confused due to challenges in high-power cytology differentiation; and non-tumor structures (elastosis, vessels, nerves) were often misclassified as dermis.

The application of this deep learning model is recommended only as a diagnostic supplement and should always be conducted under the supervision of an expert pathologist to avoid potentially harmful consequences.
The study addresses the common issue in previous deep learning studies by providing a publicly available curated dataset and code, enabling external validation and improvement of models.
The internal dataset was derived from institutions in Germany (Heidelberg, Trier, Hannover), which may limit generalizability to other populations or scanning protocols without external validation.
